name: Stream Chat API
what_it_does: Stream Chat provides a scalable in-app chat infrastructure API with REST for management and WebSocket for real-time message delivery, supporting channels, threads, reactions, user presence, and moderation — with SDKs for most platforms.
use_cases: Create and configure chat channels for users, then monitor new message events via webhook to trigger agent workflows
Send automated messages or bot replies into channels using the server-side API with a bot user token
Retrieve message history from a channel for analysis, summarization, or compliance archiving
Manage user moderation actions (mute, ban, shadow-ban) in response to content policy violations detected by an agent
Create and update user profiles, assign roles, and control channel membership as part of a user onboarding automation
not_for: Email or push notification delivery — Stream Chat handles in-app messaging only; separate notification services are needed for email or mobile push
Asynchronous task queuing or event streaming beyond chat message delivery
High-volume machine-to-machine data pipelines — Stream Chat is optimized for human chat UX, not high-throughput automated message buses
best_when: An agent needs to send or receive messages in a user-facing chat interface, operate as a chat bot, or monitor and moderate chat activity in an application built on Stream Chat.
avoid_when: You are building pure backend messaging infrastructure with no user-facing chat UI — a message queue or event streaming service would be more appropriate.

auth.notes: Server-side operations use the API key + API secret pair for signing requests via HMAC-SHA256. User-facing client tokens are JWTs signed with the API secret, containing the user_id claim. Server-side SDKs handle token generation automatically. Tokens can include optional expiry for security.

pricing.free_tier_limits: 5 million messages per month free (Maker plan), up to 100 concurrent connections; no credit card required
pricing.paid_tiers: Starter at $99/month, Professional at $299/month, custom Enterprise plans; paid plans scale message volume and add features like message retention policies and advanced moderation
pricing.notes: Free tier is production-ready for small applications. Pricing scales with monthly active users (MAUs) and message volume on paid plans.

performance.rate_limits: REST API: 100 requests/second per app on free tier, higher on paid; WebSocket connections scale to millions; channel message send rate up to 100/second per channel

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Returns JSON error responses with 'code', 'message', and 'StatusCode' fields; error codes are documented and consistent; SDK exceptions wrap these cleanly for programmatic handling.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Channel creation is idempotent — creating a channel with the same ID and type returns the existing channel. Message sends are not idempotent; duplicate sends create duplicate messages. User upserts are idempotent via the UpsertUser endpoint.
known_agent_gotchas: User tokens must be generated server-side with the API secret — exposing the API secret in client code is a critical security flaw; agents providing tokens to frontend clients must generate them server-side
Channel type configuration (messaging, livestream, team, etc.) determines default permissions and message retention — choosing the wrong channel type silently applies unexpected moderation rules
Webhook delivery is at-most-once with no built-in retry — agents relying on webhooks for message processing must handle gaps by polling the REST API for missed events
Message search requires a full-text search index that must be enabled per channel type in the Stream dashboard — queries against non-indexed channels return empty results silently
Rate limits are enforced per application, not per user — a spike from one bot user counts against the app's global rate limit, affecting real user traffic

security_score_components.security_notes: HMAC-signed JWTs with user_id binding prevent token reuse across users. API secret must be kept server-side. Stream supports message retention and deletion policies for compliance. Moderation APIs support content filtering and user banning.
